pattern: Fixing the smallest of the second array's chosen values by processing indices from largest to smallest of that value turns the problem into tracking the best-sum group of a fixed size seen so far.
hints: If you fix which element supplies the minimum from the second array, only elements with a second-array value at least that large are eligible partners.
Process indices sorted by the second array's value from largest to smallest, keeping a running pool of the k largest first-array values seen so far.

Each time the pool reaches exactly k elements, its sum times the current (smallest-so-far) second-array value is a candidate answer; a min-heap keeps the pool's smallest easy to evict when a bigger first-array value arrives.

edge_inputs.description: a small mixed case with k less than the array length
k = 1 reduces to the best single product
both arrays at the maximum value with k equal to the length
k equals the length so every element must be included, no choice
the largest first-array value pairs with the smallest second-array value, tempting a greedy-by-nums1 mistake
ties in the second array force a stable choice among equal candidates

stress.description: large random arrays near the maximum length
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 100000))
    nums1 = [rng.randint(0, 100000) for _ in range(n)]
    nums2 = [rng.randint(0, 100000) for _ in range(n)]
    k = rng.randint(1, n)
    return [nums1, nums2, k]

stress.description: small random arrays, sized for differential checking against a brute-force oracle
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(2, min(size, 8))
    nums1 = [rng.randint(0, 20) for _ in range(n)]
    nums2 = [rng.randint(0, 20) for _ in range(n)]
    k = rng.randint(1, n)
    return [nums1, nums2, k]
